mnemonics: P.A.S.S. the Object: Pass an object to a method to Share its State.
keyPoints: Objects are passed by reference value, meaning the method receives a copy of the reference to the object, not a copy of the object itself.
Changes made to the object's state inside the method persist outside the method.
Reassigning the reference parameter inside the method does not affect the original reference outside the method.
This allows methods to operate on and modify the actual object, not just a disconnected copy.
commonMistakes: Believing that 'pass by reference' means reassigning the parameter will change the original variable (it doesn't).
Forgetting that primitives (int, char, etc.) are passed by value, leading to confusion when comparing behavior with objects.
Creating a new object inside the method (e.g., `param = new MyObject();`) and expecting the caller's reference to point to the new object.
